news_summary: Today's human rights progress highlights enhanced protections for children's mental health online, steps toward assisted dying access in Wales, and amplified voices of Gaza's children envisioning safety and hope.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés aujourd'hui en matière de droits humains mettent en évidence le renforcement des mesures de protection de la santé mentale des enfants en ligne, les avancées vers l'accès à l'aide à mourir au Pays de Galles et l'amplification des voix des enfants de Gaza qui aspirent à la sécurité et à l'espoir.
news_summary_es: Los avances actuales en materia de derechos humanos destacan la mejora de la protección de la salud mental de los niños en Internet, los pasos hacia el acceso a la muerte asistida en Gales y la amplificación de las voces de los niños de Gaza que anhelan seguridad y esperanza.

articles.title: EU launches action plan to tackle cyberbullying and protect children’s mental health online
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM As young people spend more of their lives online, the risks they face have become harder to ignore. From harassment in group chats to manipulated images shared without consent, cyberbullying is no longer a side issue of digital life but a central one. In response, the European Commission introduced […]
The post EU launches action plan to tackle cyberbullying and protect children’s mental health online first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

As young people spend more of their lives online, the risks they face have become harder to ignore. From harassment in group chats to manipulated images shared without consent, cyberbullying is no longer a side issue of digital life but a central one.

In response, the European Commission introduced a sweeping Action Plan Against Cyberbullying, aimed at protecting the mental health and safety of children and teenagers across the European Union. The strategy combines technology, regulation, education, and coordination among member states to build a more consistent and effective response.

At its core, the plan rests on three pillars: creating an EU-wide reporting app, coordinating national approaches, and strengthening prevention through better digital practices.

An EU-wide app to report and receive support

One of the most tangible elements of the plan is the development of a user-friendly app designed to help victims report cyberbullying quickly and safely. The app will connect directly to national helplines and support services, giving children and teenagers a clearer pathway to help.

Beyond reporting, the app is intended to allow young people to securely store and transmit evidence, a crucial step in addressing online abuse, which often disappears as quickly as it appears. The Commission will create a blueprint for the app, which member states can adapt, translate, and integrate into their national systems.

The aim is simple but powerful: no child should struggle to figure out where to turn.

A coordinated approach across the EU

While digital platforms operate across borders, protections for minors often vary from country to country. The Commission wants to close that gap by encouraging member states to develop comprehensive national plans built on a shared understanding of cyberbullying. Standardized data collection will allow countries to compare trends and measure progress more effectively.

The Action Plan also strengthens existing regulatory frameworks. Among the key steps:

Reviewing the Digital Services Act guidelines on protecting minors to reinforce platform obligations to prevent harmful content and improve reporting mechanisms.

Adopting clearer guidelines on “trusted flaggers” under the Digital Services Act, particularly for illegal cyberbullying content.

Addressing cyberbullying within video-sharing platforms as part of the ongoing evaluation of the Audiovisual Media Services Directive.

Supporting the enforcement of the Artificial Intelligence Act, especially provisions prohibiting manipulative AI practices used for bullying.

Advancing transparency rules, including labelling AI-generated content that could be misused to harass or deceive.

Together, these measures signal a shift toward stronger accountability for digital platforms.

Prevention begins with digital literacy

While enforcement matters, prevention remains a central focus. The Action Plan encourages healthier and more respectful digital habits from an early age.

The upcoming review of the Commission’s guidelines for educators will place cyberbullying prevention at the forefront of digital literacy efforts, aligning with the broader Union of Skills initiative. At the same time, the Commission plans to expand training and resources for schools through the network of Safer Internet Centres and the multilingual Better Internet for Kids platform.

Safer Internet Day, which was first launched in the EU in 2004 and now observed in approximately 160 countries, will continue to serve as a global platform promoting children’s rights and well-being both online and offline.

Why the urgency?

The numbers paint a clear picture. An estimated one in six children aged 11 to 15 report being victims of cyberbullying, and about one in eight admit to bullying others online. Meanwhile, a recent Eurobarometer survey found that more than ninety percent of Europeans believe urgent public action is needed to protect children from the negative mental health impacts of social media, cyberbullying, and age-inappropriate content.

The Action Plan reflects direct input from young people themselves. It was shaped by consultation with more than six thousand children, alongside a broader public consultation.

Building on existing protections

The plan does not start from scratch. It builds on established legislation:

The Digital Services Act requires platforms to maintain high standards of safety and privacy for minors, including tools that allow children to block or mute users and prevent being added to groups without consent.

The Audiovisual Media Services Directive mandates measures to shield minors from harmful content, including cyberbullying.

The Artificial Intelligence Act prohibits manipulative AI systems and requires labelling of deepfakes to prevent deception.

By increasing visibility and outreach for tools already available, such as the Safer Internet Centres, which served around 48 million European citizens in 2025, the Commission hopes to ensure that protections translate into practical support.

What comes next?

Implementation will involve cooperation among member states, industry leaders, civil society, international organizations, and children themselves. Additional initiatives are also underway, including piloting a privacy-preserving EU age verification solution, advancing the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act, assembling an expert panel on child online protection, and conducting an inquiry into the mental health impacts of social media.

The Commission’s message is clear: protecting children online requires not only rules, but responsiveness in systems that evolve as quickly as the digital spaces young people inhabit.

If successful, the Action Plan could mark a significant step toward making the internet safer not just in principle, but in practice.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant, concrete EU-wide initiative to combat cyberbullying and protect children's mental health online, addressing a widespread societal issue with coordinated policy, technology, and education. The plan has broad scope across the EU, involves multiple stakeholders, and aims to create tangible tools and regulatory improvements that can positively impact millions of children and teenagers, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: EU launches new plan to fight cyberbullying and protect kids online
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
More and more young people spend time online, but this also means they face risks like cyberbullying. Cyberbullying includes things like mean messages, sharing fake images, or harassment in group chats. These problems can harm children’s mental health and are becoming a major concern across Europe.

**What happened**
The European Commission introduced an Action Plan Against Cyberbullying to protect children and teenagers in all EU countries. The plan focuses on three main parts: creating an app for reporting cyberbullying, coordinating national efforts to fight it, and teaching better digital habits to prevent bullying.

The app will help young people report bullying quickly and safely. It will also let them save evidence, which is important because online abuse can disappear quickly. The app will connect to national help services across the EU.

The plan also encourages countries to work together and collect data in the same way to better understand cyberbullying. It strengthens rules for online platforms to stop harmful content and improve reporting. It also supports limits on manipulative artificial intelligence (AI) used to bully.

Finally, the plan promotes digital education for kids and teachers to build respectful online habits. It will expand training through Safer Internet Centres and use Safer Internet Day to raise awareness.

**Impact**
This plan is important because cyberbullying affects many children—about one in six kids aged 11 to 15 say they have been bullied online. It also responds to what young people themselves said in consultations. The EU’s approach combines technology, laws, and education across all member countries, making it easier for children to get help and for countries to work together.

**What's next step**
The plan will be put into action with cooperation from governments, online companies, and organizations. New projects will test ways to verify children’s ages online safely and study social media’s effects on mental health. The EU will keep updating rules to keep up with fast-changing digital spaces.

**One-sentence takeaway**
The EU’s new Action Plan brings together technology, laws, and education to better protect children from cyberbullying and support their mental health online.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’UE lance un plan d’action pour lutter contre le cyberharcèlement et protéger la santé mentale des enfants en ligne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
De plus en plus de jeunes passent du temps en ligne, mais cela les expose aussi à des risques comme le cyberharcèlement. Le cyberharcèlement comprend des messages méchants, des images truquées ou du harcèlement dans des groupes de discussion. Ces problèmes peuvent nuire à la santé mentale des enfants et sont une préoccupation importante en Europe.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
La Commission européenne a présenté un Plan d’action contre le cyberharcèlement pour protéger les enfants et les adolescents dans tous les pays de l’UE. Le plan repose sur trois points principaux : créer une application pour signaler le cyberharcèlement, coordonner les efforts nationaux, et enseigner de meilleures habitudes numériques pour prévenir le harcèlement.

L’application aidera les jeunes à signaler rapidement et en toute sécurité le harcèlement. Elle permettra aussi de conserver des preuves, ce qui est important car les abus en ligne disparaissent vite. L’application sera reliée aux services d’aide nationaux dans toute l’UE.

Le plan encourage aussi les pays à travailler ensemble et à collecter les données de la même manière pour mieux comprendre le cyberharcèlement. Il renforce les règles pour les plateformes en ligne afin d’arrêter les contenus nuisibles et d’améliorer les signalements. Il soutient aussi des limites face à l’intelligence artificielle manipulatrice utilisée pour harceler.

Enfin, le plan promeut l’éducation numérique pour les enfants et les enseignants afin de développer des comportements respectueux en ligne. Il étendra la formation via les Centres Internet Plus Sûr et utilisera la Journée pour un Internet Plus Sûr pour sensibiliser.

**Impact**
Ce plan est important car le cyberharcèlement touche beaucoup d’enfants : un enfant sur six entre 11 et 15 ans déclare en avoir été victime. Il répond aussi aux avis des jeunes eux-mêmes. L’approche de l’UE combine technologie, lois et éducation dans tous les pays membres, facilitant l’aide aux enfants et la coopération entre pays.

**Prochaine étape**
Le plan sera mis en œuvre avec la coopération des gouvernements, des entreprises en ligne et des organisations. De nouveaux projets testeront des moyens sûrs de vérifier l’âge des enfants en ligne et étudieront les effets des réseaux sociaux sur la santé mentale. L’UE continuera d’adapter les règles aux espaces numériques en évolution rapide.

**Phrase clé**
Le nouveau Plan d’action de l’UE réunit technologie, lois et éducation pour mieux protéger les enfants du cyberharcèlement et soutenir leur santé mentale en ligne.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE lanza un plan para combatir el ciberacoso y proteger la salud mental de los niños en línea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Cada vez más jóvenes pasan tiempo en internet, pero esto también los expone a riesgos como el ciberacoso. El ciberacoso incluye mensajes ofensivos, imágenes manipuladas o acoso en chats grupales. Estos problemas pueden afectar la salud mental de los niños y son una preocupación importante en Europa.

**Qué pasó**
La Comisión Europea presentó un Plan de Acción contra el ciberacoso para proteger a niños y adolescentes en todos los países de la UE. El plan se basa en tres partes principales: crear una aplicación para reportar el ciberacoso, coordinar los esfuerzos nacionales y enseñar mejores hábitos digitales para prevenir el acoso.

La aplicación ayudará a los jóvenes a reportar el acoso de forma rápida y segura. También permitirá guardar pruebas, algo importante porque los abusos en línea suelen desaparecer rápido. La app estará conectada a servicios de ayuda nacionales en toda la UE.

El plan también fomenta que los países trabajen juntos y recopilen datos de forma uniforme para entender mejor el ciberacoso. Refuerza las normas para las plataformas en línea para detener contenidos dañinos y mejorar los sistemas de reporte. Además, apoya límites para la inteligencia artificial manipuladora usada para acosar.

Por último, el plan promueve la educación digital para niños y profesores para fomentar comportamientos respetuosos en línea. Ampliará la formación a través de los Centros de Internet Seguro y usará el Día de Internet Seguro para crear conciencia.

**Impacto**
Este plan es importante porque el ciberacoso afecta a muchos niños: uno de cada seis niños entre 11 y 15 años dice haber sido víctima. También responde a la opinión de los propios jóvenes. La estrategia de la UE combina tecnología, leyes y educación en todos los países miembros, facilitando la ayuda a los niños y la cooperación entre países.

**Próximo paso**
El plan se implementará con la cooperación de gobiernos, empresas en línea y organizaciones. Nuevos proyectos probarán formas seguras de verificar la edad de los niños en línea y estudiarán los efectos de las redes sociales en la salud mental. La UE seguirá actualizando las normas para adaptarse a los rápidos cambios digitales.

**Frase clave**
El nuevo Plan de Acción de la UE une tecnología, leyes y educación para proteger mejor a los niños del ciberacoso y apoyar su salud mental en línea.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a protective network of glowing interconnected shields forming a gentle barrier around stylized abstract figures of children made from soft light, set against a calm digital landscape of softly glowing app icons and puzzle pieces fitting together, symbolizing coordinated support, reporting tools, and prevention of cyberbullying across a united community.

articles.title: Assisted dying on Welsh NHS a step closer as Senedd votes in favour
articles.summary: In major vote Senedd backs legislation which could allow for terminally ill people to end their lives.
articles.content: Despite voting against the motion, Jeremy Miles had earlier warned Senedd members that if they were to do so "there would be no powers in the bill for Welsh ministers to implement assisted dying services in Wales or to oversee or regulate such services" if the practice were legalised by the Westminster legislation.
articles.reasoning: The Senedd's vote in favor of legislation to allow assisted dying for terminally ill people represents a significant step forward in human rights and end-of-life care, potentially granting individuals greater autonomy and dignity. The decision has broad societal implications and addresses a critical ethical and legal issue with substantial impact on many people.
articles.personality_title: Welsh Parliament votes to allow assisted dying for terminally ill patients
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Wales, people who are very sick and near the end of their life currently cannot legally choose to end their life with help. The Welsh Parliament, called the Senedd, has been discussing a law that would change this.

**What happened** – Recently, the Senedd voted in favor of a new law that could allow terminally ill people to choose assisted dying. This means that those who are suffering a lot and have little time left might be able to end their lives with medical help. The vote took place despite some concerns from members like Jeremy Miles, who warned that if the UK government did not pass similar laws, Welsh ministers might not be able to manage or regulate assisted dying services properly.

**Impact** – This vote is important because it gives people who are very sick more control over how they live their last days. It also shows that Wales is taking a serious step in respecting personal choices and improving end-of-life care. For many families, this could mean less suffering and more peaceful goodbyes.

**What's next step** – Now, the law still needs to be approved fully and put into action. The Welsh government will work on the details to make sure the rules are clear and safe. They will also need to coordinate with the UK government to handle any legal challenges or differences in rules.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Welsh Parliament’s vote brings assisted dying closer to reality, offering terminally ill people the option to choose a peaceful end to their suffering under clear rules.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement gallois vote en faveur de l'aide à mourir pour les malades en phase terminale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au Pays de Galles, les personnes très malades en fin de vie ne peuvent pas légalement choisir de mettre fin à leurs jours avec de l'aide. Le Parlement gallois, appelé Senedd, discute d'une loi qui pourrait changer cela.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Récemment, le Senedd a voté en faveur d'une nouvelle loi permettant aux personnes en phase terminale de choisir l'aide à mourir. Cela signifie que ceux qui souffrent beaucoup et ont peu de temps à vivre pourraient mettre fin à leur vie avec une assistance médicale. Ce vote a eu lieu malgré les inquiétudes de certains membres comme Jeremy Miles, qui a averti que si le gouvernement britannique n'adoptait pas des lois similaires, les ministres gallois pourraient ne pas pouvoir gérer ou réglementer correctement ces services.

**Impact** – Ce vote est important car il donne aux personnes très malades plus de contrôle sur leurs derniers jours. Il montre aussi que le Pays de Galles prend une mesure sérieuse pour respecter les choix personnels et améliorer les soins en fin de vie. Pour beaucoup de familles, cela pourrait signifier moins de souffrance et des adieux plus paisibles.

**Prochaine étape** – La loi doit encore être approuvée complètement et mise en œuvre. Le gouvernement gallois travaillera sur les détails pour que les règles soient claires et sûres. Il faudra aussi coordonner avec le gouvernement britannique pour gérer tout défi légal ou différence de règles.

**Phrase clé** – Le vote du Parlement gallois rapproche l'aide à mourir de la réalité, offrant aux malades en phase terminale la possibilité de choisir une fin paisible sous des règles claires.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento galés aprueba el apoyo a la muerte asistida para pacientes terminales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Gales, las personas muy enfermas y en la etapa final de su vida no pueden legalmente elegir terminar su vida con ayuda. El Parlamento galés, llamado Senedd, ha estado discutiendo una ley que podría cambiar esto.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, el Senedd votó a favor de una nueva ley que permitiría a las personas terminales elegir la muerte asistida. Esto significa que quienes sufren mucho y tienen poco tiempo podrían terminar su vida con ayuda médica. La votación ocurrió a pesar de preocupaciones de miembros como Jeremy Miles, quien advirtió que si el gobierno del Reino Unido no aprueba leyes similares, los ministros galeses podrían no poder manejar o regular estos servicios adecuadamente.

**Impacto** – Esta votación es importante porque da a las personas muy enfermas más control sobre sus últimos días. También muestra que Gales está tomando un paso serio para respetar las decisiones personales y mejorar el cuidado al final de la vida. Para muchas familias, esto podría significar menos sufrimiento y despedidas más tranquilas.

**Próximo paso** – La ley aún debe ser aprobada completamente y puesta en práctica. El gobierno galés trabajará en los detalles para asegurar que las reglas sean claras y seguras. También deberán coordinar con el gobierno del Reino Unido para manejar posibles retos legales o diferencias en las normas.

**Frase clave** – El voto del Parlamento galés acerca la muerte asistida a la realidad, ofreciendo a los pacientes terminales la opción de elegir un final pacífico bajo reglas claras.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a serene Welsh valley at dawn, with gentle rolling hills softly illuminated by warm light, featuring a symbolic scale balanced delicately between a glowing heart and a helping hand, representing compassionate care and the careful weighing of assisted dying legislation within the Welsh NHS.

articles.title: ‘I dream of a safe life’: Gaza’s children share the future they want
articles.summary: Children in Gaza are voicing their demands for the future through a UN-run initiative that seeks to amplify their voices and restore the “fundamentals of childhood”.
articles.content:
Through expressing their hopes for peace in art, poetry and models that they have made from rubble created by the war, thousands of young people across the enclave have taken part in The Gaza We Want initiative.

With support from the UN Children’s Fund (UNICEF), the initiative has engaged children aged five to 18 to share their vision for rebuilding the territory that’s been largely destroyed since war erupted between Hamas and Israel in October 2023.

The initiative helps “reinforce the importance of meaningful, ongoing child participation in decisions about Gaza’s future,” said Jonathan Crickx, UNICEF’s head of communications in the Palestinian Occupied Territory, briefing journalists in Geneva on Tuesday.

© UNICEF A drawing shared by children in Gaza as part of the "Gaza We Want" initiative launched by UNICEF in February.

Safety first

Over 11,000 children, including children with disabilities, have participated in the initiative which asked them to “imagine dignity” and share their ideas.

Children prioritised shelter and safety; real schools with roofs, walls and toilets; followed by hospitals and psychological support; and lastly places where they can play and “reclaim what war stole from them”.

Mr. Crickx said that when thousands of children independently draw clean streets, classrooms and parks, “it is no coincidence. It is a direct appeal to the world.”

These are not extraordinary demands. They are the fundamentals of childhood - Jonathan Crickx

“The children’s deepest wish is simply the ability to sleep through the night, to walk to school without fear… I met too many children whose bodies had healed but whose fear had not.” Mr Crickx said, echoing the call for mental health support alongside physical care.

'The clarity of children'

Mr Crickx described meeting 15-year-old Hala at a temporary learning centre in the central Gaza city of Deir Al-Balah.

Months without school had harmed her education but she dreams of a safe life, a safe home, her own bedroom and a good school, he said.

Despite the ceasefire – in place since last October – more than 135 children have reportedly been killed in Gaza. UNICEF says that listening to children must be the foundation of any credible reconstruction.

“It is difficult to ignore the clarity of children who have lived through such uncertainty. A recovery that ignores children’s voices will fail them – and fail Gaza.” The UNICEF's communications chief said.

“What the Gaza children describe is not abstract. It is the Gaza they want and have the right to grow up in.”

© UNICEF A drawing shared by children in Gaza as part of the "Gaza We Want" initiative launched by UNICEF in February.

Aid continues

Relief continues for children in Gaza as fresh bread, hot meals and digital cash assistance are provided to displaced families by the UN humanitarians.

The trucking of drinking water has been stepped up after a large water line that connects Gaza with Israel shutdown two weeks ago when two leaks were identified, the UN’s humanitarian coordination office (OCHA) reported on Tuesday.

To restore sanitation levels, the UN and partners are moving quickly to rehabilitate pumping stations that were damaged or destroyed during the war. This is critical as sewage overflows can lead to water contamination and spread of waterborne diseases.

Although two new clinics for the screening of non-communicable diseases have become operational in North Gaza, there continues to be a critical shortage of medicine and laboratory supplies, particularly for cancer and heart diseases.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a UNICEF initiative amplifying the voices of over 11,000 children in Gaza, focusing on their hopes for safety, education, and mental health amidst conflict. This represents a meaningful humanitarian effort with broad societal impact, emphasizing child participation in rebuilding and recovery, which is inspiring and hopeful despite ongoing challenges.
articles.personality_title: Gaza’s children speak out on the safe and hopeful future they want
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gaza has faced severe destruction and hardship since conflict erupted in October 2023. Many children there have experienced loss, fear, and disruption to their daily lives, including school closures and damaged homes.

**What happened** – Over 11,000 children aged 5 to 18 in Gaza took part in a UNICEF-led project called The Gaza We Want. They used art, poetry, and models made from rubble to share their hopes and ideas for rebuilding their homes and communities. This project invited children to express what they need most, such as safe shelters, real schools, hospitals, and places to play.

**Impact** – The children’s messages are clear and simple: they want safety, education, and mental health support. Their drawings of clean streets and parks are a strong call for peace and normal life. UNICEF says listening to children is essential for successful recovery and rebuilding efforts. The project shows that children’s voices matter deeply in shaping the future of Gaza.

**What’s next step** – UNICEF and partners will continue to provide aid like food, water, and health services while working to repair damaged infrastructure. They will also keep involving children in planning Gaza’s recovery to ensure their needs are met. This ongoing participation aims to help build a safer and healthier environment for Gaza’s young people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Gaza’s children are sharing their urgent need for safety, schooling, and healing through a UNICEF initiative that puts their voices at the center of rebuilding their future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les enfants de Gaza expriment leur souhait d’un avenir sûr et plein d’espoir
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Gaza a subi de lourdes destructions et difficultés depuis le conflit d’octobre 2023. Beaucoup d’enfants ont vécu la perte, la peur et la rupture de leur quotidien, notamment avec la fermeture des écoles et la destruction des maisons.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Plus de 11 000 enfants âgés de 5 à 18 ans ont participé à un projet mené par l’UNICEF appelé « La Gaza que nous voulons ». Ils ont utilisé l’art, la poésie et des maquettes faites avec des débris pour partager leurs espoirs et idées pour reconstruire leurs foyers et communautés. Ce projet leur a demandé ce dont ils ont le plus besoin, comme des abris sûrs, de vraies écoles, des hôpitaux et des lieux pour jouer.

**Impact** – Les messages des enfants sont clairs et simples : ils veulent la sécurité, l’éducation et un soutien psychologique. Leurs dessins de rues propres et de parcs sont un appel fort à la paix et à la vie normale. L’UNICEF affirme que l’écoute des enfants est essentielle pour réussir la reconstruction. Ce projet montre que la voix des enfants est cruciale pour imaginer l’avenir de Gaza.

**Prochaine étape** – L’UNICEF et ses partenaires continueront à fournir de l’aide comme la nourriture, l’eau et les soins de santé, tout en réparant les infrastructures endommagées. Ils continueront aussi à impliquer les enfants dans la planification de la reconstruction pour répondre à leurs besoins. Cette participation vise à créer un environnement plus sûr et plus sain pour les jeunes de Gaza.

**En une phrase** – Les enfants de Gaza expriment leur besoin urgent de sécurité, d’école et de soutien à travers une initiative de l’UNICEF qui place leur voix au cœur de la reconstruction de leur avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Los niños de Gaza expresan su deseo de un futuro seguro y esperanzador
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Gaza ha sufrido graves daños y dificultades desde que estalló el conflicto en octubre de 2023. Muchos niños han experimentado pérdidas, miedo y la interrupción de su vida diaria, incluyendo el cierre de escuelas y la destrucción de hogares.

**Qué pasó** – Más de 11,000 niños de entre 5 y 18 años participaron en un proyecto dirigido por UNICEF llamado La Gaza que Queremos. Usaron arte, poesía y modelos hechos con escombros para compartir sus esperanzas e ideas para reconstruir sus hogares y comunidades. El proyecto les pidió que expresaran qué necesitan más, como refugios seguros, escuelas reales, hospitales y lugares para jugar.

**Impacto** – Los mensajes de los niños son claros y sencillos: quieren seguridad, educación y apoyo psicológico. Sus dibujos de calles limpias y parques son un fuerte llamado a la paz y a la vida normal. UNICEF dice que escuchar a los niños es esencial para el éxito en la recuperación. El proyecto muestra que la voz de los niños es fundamental para imaginar el futuro de Gaza.

**Próximo paso** – UNICEF y sus socios continuarán proporcionando ayuda como alimentos, agua y servicios de salud mientras reparan la infraestructura dañada. También seguirán involucrando a los niños en la planificación de la recuperación para asegurar que sus necesidades sean atendidas. Esta participación continua busca crear un ambiente más seguro y saludable para los jóvenes de Gaza.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los niños de Gaza están compartiendo su urgente necesidad de seguridad, escuela y apoyo a través de una iniciativa de UNICEF que pone sus voces en el centro de la reconstrucción de su futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a cluster of colorful paper houses and small handmade models crafted from rubble, gently illuminated by soft natural light, surrounded by scattered children's drawings of safe schools, playgrounds, and hospitals, all set against a calm, abstract backdrop symbolizing hope and rebuilding in Gaza.

articles.title: Pop-up journals for policy research: can temporary titles deliver answers?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 February 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00446-7Journals that focus on specific research questions could help to bridge the science–policy gap, if they can attract researchers.
articles.content:
Credit: Liudmila Chernetska/iStock via Getty

Policy-relevant research is drowning in the flood of scientific papers that are published every day. But what if temporary ‘pop up’ journals that were devoted to a single, urgent question could deliver clear, actionable information straight to the policymakers who need it?

That’s the thinking behind the Pop-Up Journal Initiative, which aims to connect policymakers looking for solid evidence to back fresh policies, with researchers who are collecting the relevant evidence.

With funding of some US$1 million from two non-profit organizations — the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation in New York City and Coefficient Giving in San Francisco, California — the initiative will set up journals that will publish articles focused on a single question for a period of time, roughly a few years, before closing the pop-up journal to submissions.

“I’m less interested in topics than in questions, and I’m less interested in publishing than I am in curation,” says one of the project leaders, Daniel Goroff, who is the vice-president and programme director at the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation.

The first pop‑up journal is set to begin publishing this year and will concentrate on a classic economics question: how much growth does investment in research and development (R&D) actually generate?

Goroff says this kind of question is often asked in high-level government discussions, but clear answers are often scarce.

“When I’ve testified before [US] Congress or dealt with an appropriations bill or a budget negotiation, this question, of what is the return on investments when you’re doing R&D, comes up quite often,” says Goroff. “It’s been asked by economists in very formal ways since at least the 1950s, but the data and the methods that were available were really not very strong.”

Most researchers would jump at the chance to get their paper in the hands of the right policymaker — but would they submit their work to a journal that stops publishing after a few years, instead of a more prestigious title?

How it works

Each pop-up journal will be run by an organization, such as a research institution or an academic publisher. The organizations will be selected through a competitive bidding process run by the Pop-Up Journal Initiative’s advisory committee, which currently comprises US and UK academics and economists.

The committee will choose the journal’s central question, but organizations are free to pitch ideas, says Matt Clancy, a programme director at Coefficient Giving, who is based in Des Moines, Iowa. “We want to take bids from all corners, because you never quite know who will have a great idea.”

There are two funding streams: one for the organizations that run the pop-up journals, and another for researchers or institutions that want to conduct studies to submit to the journal.

Nature Index 2025 Research Leaders

Each journal will have a steering committee that coordinates projects, measures progress and, in some cases, commissions papers. The committees will also produce essays that summarize the findings in the papers, as well as from other relevant research, says Goroff.
articles.reasoning: The article describes an innovative initiative to create temporary 'pop-up' journals focused on urgent policy questions, aiming to bridge the science-policy gap by delivering actionable research directly to policymakers. This has broad societal significance as it can improve evidence-based policymaking, benefiting many people. The article provides sufficient detail about the initiative's funding, structure, and goals, making it a substantive and inspiring development in science communication and policy impact.
articles.personality_title: New pop-up journals aim to deliver quick answers to urgent policy questions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Policymakers often need clear and reliable scientific answers to important questions, but they struggle to find them because so many research papers are published every day. This makes it hard to pick out the most useful information.

**What happened** – A new project called the Pop-Up Journal Initiative will create temporary journals focused on one urgent question at a time. These journals will publish research for a few years before closing. The first journal will explore how much economic growth comes from investing in research and development (R&D). The project is funded with about $1 million from two non-profit organizations, the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation and Coefficient Giving.

**Impact** – This idea is unique because it focuses on a single important question and connects researchers directly with policymakers who need clear answers. Instead of publishing many unrelated studies, these journals will carefully select research that helps decision-makers. For example, understanding the return on investment in R&D can influence government budgets and policies.

**What's next step** – The Pop-Up Journal Initiative will choose organizations to run these journals through a competition. Each journal will have a steering committee to guide research and share summaries of the findings. The first pop-up journal will start publishing this year, and other journals on different topics could follow.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Temporary pop-up journals focused on key questions could make it easier for policymakers to find and use the best scientific evidence when making decisions.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux journaux éphémères pour répondre rapidement aux questions urgentes des politiques publiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les décideurs ont souvent besoin de réponses scientifiques claires et fiables à des questions importantes, mais ils ont du mal à les trouver car tant d’articles de recherche sont publiés chaque jour. Cela rend difficile la sélection des informations les plus utiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un nouveau projet appelé Pop-Up Journal Initiative va créer des journaux temporaires consacrés à une question urgente à la fois. Ces journaux publieront des recherches pendant quelques années avant de fermer. Le premier journal portera sur la croissance économique générée par les investissements en recherche et développement (R&D). Le projet est financé à hauteur d’environ 1 million de dollars par deux organisations à but non lucratif, la fondation Alfred P. Sloan et Coefficient Giving.

**Impact** – Cette idée est unique car elle se concentre sur une question importante et met en relation directe les chercheurs avec les décideurs qui ont besoin de réponses claires. Au lieu de publier de nombreuses études non liées, ces journaux sélectionneront soigneusement les recherches qui aident les décideurs. Par exemple, comprendre le retour sur investissement en R&D peut influencer les budgets et les politiques gouvernementales.

**Prochaine étape** – L’initiative choisira les organisations qui géreront ces journaux via un concours. Chaque journal aura un comité de pilotage pour guider les recherches et partager des résumés des résultats. Le premier journal éphémère commencera à publier cette année, et d’autres journaux sur différents sujets pourraient suivre.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Des journaux éphémères centrés sur des questions clés pourraient faciliter l’accès des décideurs aux meilleures preuves scientifiques pour prendre leurs décisions.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas revistas temporales buscan ofrecer respuestas rápidas a preguntas urgentes de políticas públicas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los responsables de políticas públicas suelen necesitar respuestas científicas claras y fiables a preguntas importantes, pero les cuesta encontrarlas porque se publican muchos artículos de investigación cada día. Esto dificulta encontrar la información más útil.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo proyecto llamado Pop-Up Journal Initiative creará revistas temporales centradas en una pregunta urgente a la vez. Estas revistas publicarán investigaciones durante unos años antes de cerrar. La primera revista tratará sobre cuánto crecimiento económico genera la inversión en investigación y desarrollo (I+D). El proyecto cuenta con unos 1 millón de dólares en financiación de dos organizaciones sin fines de lucro, la Fundación Alfred P. Sloan y Coefficient Giving.

**Impacto** – Esta idea es única porque se enfoca en una sola pregunta importante y conecta directamente a los investigadores con los responsables de políticas que necesitan respuestas claras. En lugar de publicar muchos estudios sin relación, estas revistas seleccionarán cuidadosamente investigaciones que ayuden a los tomadores de decisiones. Por ejemplo, entender el retorno de la inversión en I+D puede influir en los presupuestos y políticas gubernamentales.

**Próximo paso** – La iniciativa elegirá las organizaciones que dirigirán estas revistas mediante una competencia. Cada revista tendrá un comité que guiará la investigación y compartirá resúmenes de los hallazgos. La primera revista temporal comenzará a publicar este año, y podrían seguir otras sobre diferentes temas.

**Frase clave** – Las revistas temporales centradas en preguntas clave podrían facilitar que los responsables de políticas encuentren y usen la mejor evidencia científica para tomar decisiones.
